title: Ring
description: Youssef Rakha on mourning rites: boxing, poetry, and witnessing Gaza.
hook: Boxing, poetry, and the act of witnessing—how mourning rites become a way to hold something impossible in your body.

title: 'Art Isn't Easy' author offers new insights into Stephen Sondheim's life and music
description: Biographer Daniel Okrent discusses Sondheim's approach to writing music and lyrics, his often toxic relationship with his mother and his work with mentors and collaborators.
hook: A toxic mother, impossible collaborators, and the obsessive perfectionism that made Sondheim's sentences sing—a biography of ambition's true cost.

title: I Can Imagine It for Us: Mai Serhan on Palestine & the Politics of Storytelling
description: Blending poetry, archival research, and fragments of family history, Mai Serhan writes from post-memory to conjure a homeland she has never seen.
hook: She writes from a place she's never been, stitching poetry and archival fragments into an imagined homeland—post-memory as political act.

title: Pulling Bolts Out of the Ferris Wheel
description: The mere existence of a second Mary Bronstein movie, much less one as amazing as this, proves that patience and a bad attitude are not just their own rewards. If I Had Legs I’d Kick You, her second film and her first since 2008’s Yeast (one of the best American films of the 21st century), is a masterpiece of alienated frenzy. It’s a comic version of The Exorcist in which the mother
hook: A second Mary Bronstein film exists, and it's amazing—proof that bad attitudes and patience eventually get rewarded by art nobody expected.

title: Sylvia Plath meditated in hers. Frida Kahlo painted her whole life floating in one. Is the bathtub a political object?
description: Sylvia Plath meditated in hers. Frida Kahlo painted her whole life floating in one. Is the bathtub a political object?
hook: Plath meditated in hers, Kahlo painted in hers—the bathtub as confessional, mirror, and site of female refusal.
